findings.summary: CaptureSettings is a large mutable class with 30+ properties covering video, audio, HDR, flashback, encoding, and output path concerns. It is built in the ViewModel, mutated in-place by CaptureService.UpdateFlashbackSettings(), stored as _currentSettings, and passed through session coordinator methods. Each consumer uses a small subset: FlashbackEncoderSink needs 6-7 encoding properties, the recording pipeline needs format/quality/bitrate, and the preview system needs resolution/fps/HDR mode. The class acts as a shared mutable bag rather than domain-specific parameter objects.
evidence: CaptureSettings has 30+ public mutable properties spanning video capture, audio routing, HDR metadata, flashback config, and MJPEG decode
CaptureService.UpdateFlashbackSettings() mutates _currentSettings.FlashbackBufferMinutes and FlashbackGpuDecode in-place after construction
FlashbackSessionContext is a focused record with ~15 fields â€” the right shape, but CaptureSettings itself remains unfactored

findings.summary: CaptureSessionCoordinator serializes operations onto a work queue, which provides genuine value (ordering guarantees). However, the public API methods (InitializeAsync, StartVideoPreviewAsync, etc.) are pure pass-throughs that each create a lambda forwarding to the corresponding CaptureService method with no added translation, validation, or policy. The serialization value is real but lives entirely in the private EnqueueAsync infrastructure â€” the 10+ public methods are boilerplate wrappers.
evidence: Lines 103-122: each public method follows the identical pattern: EnqueueAsync(CommandKind.X, ct => _captureService.XAsync(args, ct), cancellationToken) with zero added logic
UpdateAudioMonitoringAsync (lines 125-140) is the one exception that adds meaningful orchestration logic

findings.summary: IAutomationViewModel has ~35 methods spanning device selection, capture settings, HDR, audio, preview/recording, UI panels, flashback, and probes. It acts as a single facade over the entire application surface for the automation layer. The interface provides a genuine boundary (Services defines it, ViewModels implements it), but the breadth means the automation layer has direct knowledge of every feature. Each new feature requires extending this single interface.
evidence: Interface spans 90 lines with 35+ members across 9 conceptual groups
AutomationCommandDispatcher.ExecuteAsync switch statement handles 30+ command kinds, each calling a different IAutomationViewModel method

findings.summary: Several XML doc comments restate what the method name and signature already communicate, adding verbosity without insight. This pattern appears across the Flashback subsystem and some model types.
evidence: FlashbackBufferManager.UpdateLatestPts: '/// Called by the encoder on each video frame to update the latest PTS.' â€” the method name says this
FlashbackExporter line 257: '/// Sets the file extension for new segments (e.g. ".ts" or ".mp4").' on SetSegmentExtension(string extension) â€” restates the signature

FlashbackPlaybackController.UpdateAudioComponents: doc says 'Updates audio references after WASAPI components become available' â€” the name communicates this, but the follow-up sentence about ordering is genuinely useful

findings.summary: The Flashback subsystem has very dense structured diagnostic logging â€” almost every state transition, eviction, segment lifecycle event, and error path is logged with key=value pairs. While individual log lines are purposeful (they enable live diagnostics via MCP), the aggregate volume is high. For instance, FlashbackBufferManager logs on Initialize, every segment complete, every eviction cycle, every purge, dispose, and disk eviction. FlashbackPlaybackController logs state transitions, thread start/stop, scrub events, and audio suppression. This is well-organized but borders on noisy.
evidence: FlashbackBufferManager has 12+ Logger.Log calls for lifecycle, eviction, segment tracking, dispose, PTS update logging
FlashbackPlaybackController logs every command dispatch (FLASHBACK_PLAYBACK_CMD_DROP), every state change, thread lifecycle, and scrub begin/end
FlashbackEncoderSink logs queue initialization, GPU encoding state, and start context

findings.summary: CaptureDiagnosticsSnapshot has 95+ properties as a flat init-only class. CaptureHealthSnapshot inherits and adds another 30+ properties. These snapshot objects are constructed by populating every field individually in GetHealthSnapshot(). While each field serves a diagnostic purpose, the shape resembles LLM-generated exhaustive property coverage rather than a designed diagnostic model. Fields like MjpegReorderSampleCount, MjpegReorderAvgMs, MjpegReorderP95Ms, MjpegReorderMaxMs repeat the same statistical pattern 6+ times for different pipeline stages.
evidence: CaptureDiagnosticsSnapshot: 115 lines of init-only properties with 6 repetitions of the (SampleCount, AvgMs, P95Ms, MaxMs) pattern for different Mjpeg pipeline stages
The same 4-field timing pattern appears for: MjpegDecode, MjpegInteropCopy, MjpegCallback, MjpegReorder, MjpegPipeline, CaptureCadence â€” a LatencyMetrics struct could deduplicate this
CaptureHealthSnapshot adds another 30+ fields as flat properties

findings.summary: The top-level package structure maps well to domain capabilities. Services/Flashback groups buffer management, encoding, decoding, playback, and export. Services/Capture owns device lifecycle. Services/Recording contains encoder and sink contracts. Services/Automation separates the IPC boundary. ViewModels uses partial classes to split coherent concerns (Capture lifecycle, Settings, DeviceManagement, Automation, AudioControls, Telemetry). Models contains enums, settings, snapshots, and domain records. The separation is predictable â€” a new developer could find where flashback export lives without searching.
evidence: 5 Flashback files with clear single responsibilities: BufferManager, EncoderSink, Decoder, PlaybackController, Exporter
ViewModel split into 7 partial files each with a focused concern
RecordingContracts.cs groups all recording-related interfaces and types in one place

findings.summary: MainWindow is split across 10+ partial class files (Flashback, PropertyChanged, Bindings, PreviewStartup, PreviewRenderer, Animations, AudioMeter, Screenshot, FullScreen, StatsOverlay, EventHandlers, xaml.cs). While individual files are focused, the aggregate surface means MainWindow owns animation, flashback timeline UI, preview lifecycle, audio meter painting, stats overlay, screenshot capture, fullscreen management, and all property-change UI dispatch. This is a WinUI 3 code-behind reality, but the window has accumulated significant behavioral responsibility.
evidence: MainWindow.PropertyChanged.cs handles every ViewModel property change via a giant switch/case tree that dispatches to UI updates
MainWindow.Flashback.cs manages timeline animation, scrub gesture handling, position UI updates, and playback polling timers â€” 200+ lines of flashback-specific UI logic
10+ partial files for a single class indicates accumulated responsibility

findings.summary: The ViewModel.Automation partial class uses a clean TryGetActiveFlashback() guard that provides NotNullWhen annotation and checks both existence and non-disabled state. All 7 flashback command methods (BeginScrub, UpdateScrub, EndScrub, Play, Pause, GoLive, Nudge) use this consistently. This is a well-crafted handoff pattern that centralizes the null/state check.
evidence: TryGetActiveFlashback uses [NotNullWhen(true)] for type narrowing
7 methods follow the identical if (!TryGetActiveFlashback(out var c)) return false; c.DoThing(); return true; pattern
Clean replacement for what would otherwise be 7 copies of null-check + state-check boilerplate

findings.summary: FlashbackExportRequest cleanly bundles export parameters (SegmentPaths, InputTsPath, InPoint, OutPoint, OutputPath, FastStart) into a single record with required properties. The ExportAsync method dispatches between single-file and multi-segment export based on whether SegmentPaths is populated. This avoids a 7-parameter method signature and makes the export contract explicit.
evidence: FlashbackExportRequest uses 'required' keyword for InPoint, OutPoint, OutputPath â€” compile-time contract enforcement
ExportAsync dispatches on SegmentPaths presence rather than requiring callers to choose between two API shapes

findings.summary: BuildCaptureSettings() in MainViewModel.Capture.cs is a 150-line method that resolves frame rate from 5 different sources (selectedFrameRateOption, runtime snapshot, source telemetry, selected format, effective frame rate) with multiple fallback chains and conditional overwrites. The frame rate resolution logic cascades through: user selection -> runtime match -> source telemetry match -> format fallback -> hardcoded 60. Each step has its own rational/numerator/denominator resolution path. This is complex domain logic that works, but the multiple mutation points make it hard to trace which source ultimately wins.
evidence: Lines 275-425: 150 lines for one method with 5 frame rate source candidates and 4 conditional overwrite blocks
requestedFrameRateArg, requestedFrameRateNumerator, requestedFrameRateDenominator are mutated through 4 successive if-blocks
The method simultaneously resolves resolution, frame rate, quality, codec, and audio config into a single CaptureSettings object

findings.summary: CaptureService uses RunTransitionAsync() to wrap state-changing operations with the session transition lock and state management. RestartFlashbackAsync and UpdateRecordingFormatAsync both use this pattern cleanly: RunTransitionAsync(_sessionState, async transitionToken => { ... }, cancellationToken). This provides consistent lifecycle management at module boundaries.
evidence: RestartFlashbackAsync (line 161) and UpdateRecordingFormatAsync (line 188) both use RunTransitionAsync with the same shape
Pattern centralizes _sessionTransitionLock acquisition and CancellationToken linking

findings.summary: FlashbackBufferManager demonstrates disciplined concurrent state management. PTS tracking uses lock-free Interlocked CAS for the hot path (UpdateLatestPts), while structural changes (segment add/remove, initialization, eviction) use a single _indexLock. The UpdateLatestPts method properly uses a CAS loop for monotonic update, then escalates to the lock only when eviction is needed and double-checks the eviction pause flag under the lock to close the TOCTOU window. This is precise without being over-engineered.
evidence: UpdateLatestPts lines 517-554: lock-free CAS for PTS, then lock-guarded eviction check with a TOCTOU guard comment
BufferedDuration property reads two atomics without lock â€” correct because the invariant (latest >= start) is maintained by monotonic updates
Eviction has clean two-phase approach: time-based eviction then disk-based eviction, both under the same lock

findings.summary: The FlashbackExporter.ExportCore method uses a well-structured two-phase packet processing approach: Phase 1 buffers packets until all stream timestamp bases are discovered (capped at 600 packets to handle silent streams), then Phase 2 processes remaining packets inline. The timestamp rebasing uses a global minimum base in microseconds rescaled per-stream. The approach correctly handles edge cases: EOF during Phase 1, partially discovered bases, silent audio streams, and cancellation during buffering.
evidence: Lines 206-370: Phase 1/Phase 2 approach with explicit allBasesDiscovered flag and MaxBufferedPackets=600 safety cap
finally block (lines 358-370) properly frees any buffered packets on early exit/error/cancellation
Phase 1 EOF handling (lines 342-355) gracefully handles missing stream bases by defaulting to 0

findings.summary: FlashbackPlaybackController uses an UnboundedChannel<PlaybackCommand> with a dedicated playback thread that alternates between TryRead (non-blocking during playback) and WaitToReadAsync (blocking when paused). The command enum (BeginScrub, UpdateScrub, EndScrub, Play, Pause, GoLive, Nudge, Stop) cleanly models all valid transitions. The UpdateScrub handler drains stale commands to process only the latest position (F2 fix). State restoration after scrub (_wasPlayingBeforeScrub) handles the M16 edge case. The playback thread uses timeBeginPeriod(1) for 1ms sleep resolution at 120fps â€” a necessary Windows-specific optimization.
evidence: Lines 332-362: dual-mode command consumption â€” TryRead during playback for non-blocking, WaitToReadAsync when paused
Lines 399: while (_commandChannel.Reader.TryRead(out var newer)) drains stale UpdateScrub commands
timeBeginPeriod(1) call at thread entry ensures Thread.Sleep accuracy for 120fps pacing

findings.summary: MainViewModel.cs declares 30+ private backing fields for state flags (_isChangingDevice, _suppressFormatChangeReinitialize, _isRevertingHdrToggle, _isLoadingSettings, _isRefreshingDeviceAudioControls, _isApplyingAutomaticFrameRateSelection, etc.) alongside 15+ pending-state fields (_pendingSavedDeviceId, _pendingSavedAudioDeviceId, _pendingSavedMicrophoneDeviceId, etc.). The naming is consistent and each flag has a clear purpose, but the sheer count indicates the ViewModel manages many overlapping state machines (device change, settings load, format change, HDR toggle, telemetry update) within a single object.
evidence: Lines 108-141: 16 boolean/nullable state flags in rapid succession, each protecting a different reentrancy or deferred-application scenario
_pendingSavedDeviceId through _pendingSavedAnalogAudioGainPercent (lines 131-140): 8 deferred-restore fields that exist because device population is async

findings.summary: MediaFormat.Equals/GetHashCode is correctly implemented with a nuanced frame rate comparison: when both instances have rational numerator/denominator, it compares exactly; otherwise it falls back to floating-point comparison with 0.01 tolerance. GetHashCode correctly mirrors this by branching on the same rational-availability condition. The double-rounding in the floating-point hash path (Math.Round(FrameRateExact, 3)) is slightly inconsistent with the 0.01 tolerance in Equals (rounding to 3 decimal places is finer than 0.01), but this is a minor theoretical concern.
evidence: Equals uses Math.Abs(FrameRateExact - other.FrameRateExact) < 0.01 tolerance
GetHashCode uses Math.Round(FrameRateExact, 3) â€” rounding to 0.001 when equality uses 0.01 means two values that are Equals (e.g., 59.994 and 59.995) could have different hash codes
The rational path is clean and correct: exact integer comparison

findings.summary: In FlashbackTools, flashback_action uses the shared ExecuteAndFormatAsync helper (clean), but flashback_export and flashback_segments manually construct response strings with StringBuilder, duplicating the status/message extraction pattern that ExecuteAndFormatAsync already handles. This is the kind of inconsistency where some tools adopted the deduplication pattern and others didn't.
evidence: flashback_action (line 30) uses ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync â€” the consolidated pattern
flashback_export (lines 57-71) manually calls pipeClient.SendCommandAsync, extracts status/message, builds StringBuilder â€” bypasses the shared helper
flashback_segments (lines 76-89) repeats the same manual pattern

findings.summary: All 20 MCP tool classes now use a consistent formatting layer. Tools that batch multiple operations use ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync, while tools that need custom output (FlashbackTools.flashback_export, VerificationTools, DiagnosticsTools) use AutomationSnapshotFormatter.IsSuccess/Get directly. Both paths use the same underlying AutomationSnapshotFormatter from the Common project. ResponseFormatter has been deleted. This is coherent.
evidence: FlashbackTools line 30: uses ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync for flashback_action
FlashbackTools line 58: uses AutomationSnapshotFormatter.IsSuccess/Get directly for flashback_export (custom format needed)
CaptureSettingsTools, PipelineSettingsTools, UiSettingsTools, DeviceTools, WindowTools all use ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync
RecordingTools, PreviewTools use AutomationSnapshotFormatter directly for simple single-command responses
No ResponseFormatter.cs exists in the McpServer project
findings.suggestion: RecordingTools and PreviewTools could use ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync for full consistency (they do the same status+message formatting inline), but this is a minor nit.

findings.summary: FlashbackExporter has a clean 3-param public API via FlashbackExportRequest record. ExportAsync(FlashbackExportRequest, IProgress<ExportProgress>?, CancellationToken) routes internally to single-file or multi-segment export. The request record groups 5 related parameters into a semantic unit with 'required' annotations on the non-optional fields.
evidence: FlashbackExportRequest: SegmentPaths (optional), InputTsPath (optional), required InPoint, required OutPoint, required OutputPath, FastStart=true default
ExportAsync line 30-41: single public entry, dispatches based on request.SegmentPaths presence
findings.suggestion: None. This is a strong API pattern.

findings.summary: AutomationCommandDispatcher returns AutomationCommandResponse with consistent structure across all 45+ commands. Two helper methods (CreateResponse and CreateAcknowledgedResponse) ensure uniform shape. All success responses include a snapshot. All error responses include errorCode and status='error'. The response shape is predictable.
evidence: CreateResponse (line 594-620): uniform factory with correlationId, message, data, errorCode, success, status, commandLifecycle, retryAfterMs, elapsedMs, optional snapshot
CreateAcknowledgedResponse (line 622-635): delegates to CreateResponse with lifecycle='acknowledged'
All 45 switch cases use one of these two helpers

findings.summary: RecordingTools.control_recording and PreviewTools.control_preview format responses inline with the same pattern (IsSuccess+Get+string interpolation) instead of using ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync. This is functionally identical but diverges from the pattern used by all other simple setter tools.
evidence: RecordingTools line 22-24: manual IsSuccess/Get pattern
PreviewTools line 22-24: identical manual pattern
Compare to WindowTools line 45: uses ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync
findings.suggestion: Migrate to ToolCommandFormatter.ExecuteAndFormatAsync for consistency. Each would be a 1-line call.

findings.summary: IAutomationViewModel is cleanly segmented with comment headers (Properties, Snapshot/diagnostic queries, Device selection, Capture settings, HDR, Audio, Preview/recording/output, UI panels, Flashback, Probes). All async methods consistently take CancellationToken. Flashback control methods are synchronous (bool return) which is appropriate for their fire-and-forget nature. No mixed sync/async for the same concern.
evidence: Lines 17-90: 10 sections, each clearly labeled
All setting methods: Task return + CancellationToken
Flashback methods (lines 77-83): bool for play/pause/seek (UI thread, immediate), Task for export (long-running)

findings.summary: All automation commands pass through a single auth gate in AutomationCommandDispatcher.ExecuteAsync (line 52). The IsAuthorized method (line 942-956) checks _authToken against the request's AuthToken or payload authToken. When no token is configured, all requests pass (open mode for local development). This is consistently applied -- no command can bypass the gate.
evidence: AutomationCommandDispatcher line 52: var authorized = IsAuthorized(request) -- before command switch
Line 53-75: Authenticate command returns auth status; all other commands reject if unauthorized
AutomationPipeProtocol.GetConfiguredAuthToken (line 65-74): shared across all tool clients
PipeClient, PipeTransport, AutomationClient all use CreateRequestEnvelope which includes auth token
findings.suggestion: None. Auth pattern is correctly centralized.

findings.summary: The automation token environment variable name 'ELGATOCAPTURE_AUTOMATION_TOKEN' is defined as a constant in AutomationPipeProtocol.AutomationKeyEnvVar, but MainWindow.xaml.cs (line 291) and MainWindow.WindowManagement.cs (line 76) read it via raw Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable with a string literal instead of using the constant. The MainWindow doesn't reference the Common project, so the constant isn't directly accessible, but the string duplication means a rename would require updating 3 locations.
evidence: AutomationPipeProtocol line 8: internal const string AutomationKeyEnvVar = "ELGATOCAPTURE_AUTOMATION_TOKEN";
MainWindow.xaml.cs line 291: Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("ELGATOCAPTURE_AUTOMATION_TOKEN")
MainWindow.WindowManagement.cs line 76: Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("ELGATOCAPTURE_AUTOMATION_TOKEN")
MainWindow.WindowManagement.cs line 77: Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("ELGATOCAPTURE_AUTOMATION_PIPE") -- pipe name also duplicated
findings.suggestion: Define a small constant (e.g., in NamedPipeAutomationServer or a constants class) for the env var names within the ElgatoCapture assembly. Or reference AutomationPipeProtocol.AutomationKeyEnvVar if the Common project is available.

findings.summary: The WindowAction.Close command has a specific two-step safety gate: ArmClose must be called first, then WindowAction(Close). This prevents accidental window closure via automation. The arm state is tracked with a lock and reset after use. This is a well-designed authorization sub-pattern for a destructive operation.
evidence: Lines 379-387: ArmClose sets _closeArmed = true under lock
Lines 389-428: WindowAction(Close) checks _closeArmed under lock, resets to false, rejects if not armed with errorCode 'window-close-not-armed'
findings.suggestion: None. This is a good safety pattern.

findings.summary: Previously, TryReadResponseState was duplicated as private methods in PipeClient, PipeTransport, and AutomationClient. All three now use AutomationResponseState.TryRead from the Common project. Migration is complete.
evidence: PipeClient line 37: AutomationResponseState.TryRead(response, out var success, out var status, out var retryAfterMs)
PipeTransport line 45: AutomationResponseState.TryRead(response, out var success, out var status, out var retryAfterMs)
AutomationClient/Program.cs line 115: return AutomationResponseState.TryRead(...)

findings.summary: ResponseFormatter.cs has been deleted from the McpServer project. All MCP tools now use AutomationSnapshotFormatter from Common (either directly or via ToolCommandFormatter). The format snapshot duplication is resolved.
evidence: No ResponseFormatter.cs exists in the McpServer project (Glob returns no results)
No references to 'ResponseFormatter' in any .cs file under tools/McpServer/
AppStateTools line 20: AutomationSnapshotFormatter.FormatSnapshot(response)

findings.summary: Two near-identical record types exist for MJPEG decoder stats: MjpegDecoderAutomationSnapshot (in AutomationContracts.cs, public) and MjpegDecoderHealthSnapshot (in CaptureHealthSnapshot.cs, public). Both have identical fields: WorkerIndex, SampleCount, AvgMs, P95Ms, MaxMs. They serve different snapshot hierarchies (AutomationSnapshot vs CaptureHealthSnapshot) but represent the same data shape.
evidence: AutomationContracts.cs line 131: record MjpegDecoderAutomationSnapshot(int WorkerIndex, int SampleCount, double AvgMs, double P95Ms, double MaxMs)
CaptureHealthSnapshot.cs line 6: record MjpegDecoderHealthSnapshot(int WorkerIndex, int SampleCount, double AvgMs, double P95Ms, double MaxMs)
AutomationDiagnosticsHub.cs line 765: constructs MjpegDecoderAutomationSnapshot
CaptureService.Snapshots.cs line 789: constructs MjpegDecoderHealthSnapshot -- same field mapping
findings.suggestion: Unify to a single type (e.g., MjpegDecoderHealthSnapshot) and use it in both snapshot hierarchies. The types are public and have identical shapes.

findings.summary: HdrOutputPolicy.cs references ELGATOCAPTURE_HDR_OUTPUT_ENABLED as a 'legacy' env var (variable name legacyEnabled on line 24-26) alongside the newer ELGATOCAPTURE_HDR_OUTPUT_FORCE_OFF. Both are still active code paths. The 'legacy' variable is still functional and not deprecated.
evidence: Line 24-26: if (EnvironmentHelpers.TryGetBoolFromEnv("ELGATOCAPTURE_HDR_OUTPUT_ENABLED", out var legacyEnabled))
The variable name 'legacyEnabled' suggests migration intent, but the env var is still checked
findings.suggestion: Either complete the migration by removing ELGATOCAPTURE_HDR_OUTPUT_ENABLED and documenting ELGATOCAPTURE_HDR_OUTPUT_FORCE_OFF as the replacement, or rename the local variable to avoid the misleading 'legacy' prefix if both vars are intentionally supported.

findings.summary: FfmpegRuntimeLocator.cs line 14 contains the comment '// -- Encoder capability probes (moved from legacy FFmpegEncoderService) --'. The move is complete -- there is no FFmpegEncoderService type anywhere in the codebase. The comment references a type that no longer exists.
evidence: Line 14: '// -- Encoder capability probes (moved from legacy FFmpegEncoderService) --'
No FFmpegEncoderService found anywhere in the codebase (grep returns 0 matches for 'FfmpegEncoderService')

This is internally consistent -- it is the project convention. The subdirectories provide navigability; the flat namespace avoids fragmented using statements.

findings.summary: The tools/Common project cleanly houses the shared automation protocol: AutomationPipeProtocol (command map, auth, timeouts), AutomationResponseState (response parsing), AutomationSnapshotFormatter (display formatting). Both McpServer and ecctl depend on Common. The command name-to-integer mapping is single-sourced in AutomationPipeProtocol.CommandMap (45 entries). This eliminates cross-tool drift.
evidence: AutomationPipeProtocol.CommandMap: single dictionary, 45 entries, case-insensitive
PipeClient.cs uses TryGetCommandValue, CreateRequestEnvelope, GetDefaultResponseTimeout from Common
PipeTransport.cs uses the same set of Common APIs
AutomationClient.cs uses AutomationResponseState.TryRead from Common

findings.summary: The 20 MCP tool classes are organized by domain concern: AppStateTools, CaptureSettingsTools, DeviceTools, DiagnosticsTools, FlashbackTools, MemoryDiagnosticsTools, PerformanceTimelineTools, PipelineSettingsTools, PreviewColorProbeTool, PreviewFrameCaptureTools, PreviewTools, RecordingTools, ToolCommandFormatter, UiSettingsTools, VerificationTools, VideoSourceProbeTool, WaitTools, WindowScreenshotTool, WindowTools. Each file maps to one or a few related automation commands. ToolCommandFormatter is a shared helper, not a tool class.

CaptureSettingsTools: resolution, frame rate, format, quality, preset, split encode, decoder count

findings.summary: The Models/ directory contains 16 files with clear domain groupings: capture types (CaptureDevice, CaptureSettings, CaptureSessionState, CaptureModeOptions), automation types (AutomationContracts), diagnostics types (CaptureDiagnosticsSnapshot, CaptureHealthSnapshot), flashback types (FlashbackModels), media types (MediaFormat, EncoderSupport), audio types (AudioInputDevice, AudioLevelEventArgs, AudioPathMode), and recording types (RecordingPipelineOptions, RecordingStats). File count is appropriate for the domain breadth.
evidence: 16 files, each focused on a specific model concern
CaptureHealthSnapshot inherits CaptureDiagnosticsSnapshot -- proper type hierarchy
FlashbackModels.cs: all flashback-specific internal types in one file

findings.summary: CaptureService.cs has ~82 private fields spanning device lifecycle, flashback state (7 fields), audio state (11 fields), recording state (8 fields), video diagnostics (15 fields), MJPEG metrics (3 fields), telemetry (2 fields), and AV sync (4 fields). While partial classes split the methods, all fields live in the primary file. The flashback subsystem alone (buffer manager, encoder sink, exporter, playback controller + their coordination fields) accounts for a significant portion.
evidence: Lines 14-91: 82 private fields across 7+ concerns
Flashback: _flashbackBufferManager, _flashbackSink, _flashbackExporter, _flashbackPlaybackController, _flashbackEnabled, _pendingFlashbackSettingsChange, _flashbackRecordingStartBytes

Audio: _wasapiAudioCapture, _microphoneCapture, _micMonitorDeviceId, _micMonitorDeviceName, _micMonitorEnabled, _wasapiAudioPlayback, _previewVolume, _isMonitoringMuted, _wasapiAudioCaptureFaulted, _wasapiAudioCaptureFaultMessage
findings.suggestion: Consider extracting flashback coordination and audio management into focused helper/manager classes that CaptureService composes. This would reduce cognitive load when working on any single concern.

findings.summary: MainViewModel is decomposed into 7 partial class files with clear responsibility labels: MainViewModel.cs (core state/collections ~100 fields), MainViewModel.Capture.cs (capture lifecycle), MainViewModel.DeviceManagement.cs (device enumeration), MainViewModel.Settings.cs (persistence), MainViewModel.Telemetry.cs (telemetry), MainViewModel.Automation.cs (IAutomationViewModel implementation), MainViewModel.AudioControls.cs (audio controls). Each file has a focused responsibility.
evidence: 7 partial files with clear domain-specific names
MainViewModel.Capture.cs line 14: doc comment 'Capture lifecycle: device initialization, preview start/stop, recording start/stop, settings builder, and device reinitialization'
MainViewModel.Settings.cs: LoadSettings, SaveSettings, format change handlers
findings.suggestion: None. This is a strong organizational pattern.

findings.summary: CaptureHealthSnapshot extends CaptureDiagnosticsSnapshot with flashback detail, source signal metadata, AV-sync data, and export info. The base class has the core diagnostics (~115 properties). The derived class adds ~40 more for full health reporting. GetDiagnosticsSnapshot() returns GetHealthSnapshot() since the full snapshot satisfies the lighter contract. This is clean type design.
evidence: CaptureHealthSnapshot.cs line 17: public sealed class CaptureHealthSnapshot : CaptureDiagnosticsSnapshot
CaptureService.Snapshots.cs line 61-65: GetDiagnosticsSnapshot() returns GetHealthSnapshot() with comment explaining Liskov substitution

findings.summary: RecordingContext has 11 forwarding properties (Settings, UsePostMuxAudio, AudioDeviceName, etc.) that delegate to Request.*. This creates a facade so existing consumers don't need to chain through context.Request.Settings. The forwarding is explicit and labeled with a comment ('Forwarding properties from Request'). The convenience D3D11/CUDA pointer accessors further simplify GPU pipeline code.
evidence: Lines 61-77: 11 forwarding properties from Request, 4 GPU handle convenience accessors
Line 60: comment '// -- Forwarding properties from Request --'
findings.suggestion: None. This is a reasonable convenience layer that reduces chaining in hot paths.

findings.summary: AutomationCommandDispatcher.ExecuteAsync contains a 470-line switch statement over 45+ AutomationCommandKind values. Each case follows a consistent pattern (extract params, call viewModel method, return response), but the sheer size makes the file ~960 lines. The consistency of each case mitigates readability concerns somewhat.
evidence: Lines 89-561: switch statement with 45+ cases
Each case: 3-8 lines, same pattern (extract payload, call viewModel, return CreateAcknowledgedResponse/CreateResponse)
FlashbackAction case (lines 232-258) is the most complex with a nested switch
findings.suggestion: The switch is large but structurally regular. A command handler map (Dictionary<AutomationCommandKind, Func<...>>) could reduce the method size, but would add indirection without improving behavior. The current approach is acceptable for a command dispatcher.
